title: Phlix server settings
description: Schema describing the editable server settings exposed by the Phlix admin settings endpoint (/api/v1/admin/settings). phlix-server derives its writable settings allow-list from this schema (AdminSettingsController::loadAllowedKeysFromSchema) and the admin SPA renders the settings form from it. Property keys are the literal dotted setting keys and MUST resolve through Phlix\Admin\SettingsRepository::getDefault(): a leading run of dotted segments names a config file under phlix-server/config/ and every remaining segment walks that file's returned array. The file part MAY span subdirectories (scrobblers.trakt.client_id resolves config/scrobblers/trakt.php) and the longest matching file path wins. A key whose path does not resolve renders an empty control and can never take effect, so no such key may be declared here. The `default` values below mirror the corresponding config/*.php literals for documentation; the GET endpoint returns the effective value (config default merged with the DB override).

hwaccel.enabled.label: Enable hardware acceleration
hwaccel.enabled.helpText: When enabled, the server will use your GPU (NVENC, QSV, VCE, or VideoToolbox) to accelerate transcoding instead of relying solely on CPU-based software encoding. This dramatically reduces CPU load and power consumption for concurrent streams, but requires a compatible GPU and appropriate driver support. If your hardware does not support acceleration, the server falls back to software encoding automatically.

hwaccel.prefer_hardware.description: Prefer hardware encoders/decoders over software when both are available.
hwaccel.prefer_hardware.label: Prefer hardware encoders/decoders
hwaccel.prefer_hardware.helpText: When both a hardware encoder/decoder and a software fallback are available for a given codec, this option causes the server to always choose the hardware path first. Disable this to prefer software encoding, which may offer better quality at the cost of higher CPU usage. Useful if you want to reserve GPU bandwidth for specific streams or if you encounter compatibility issues with hardware decoding.

transcoding.preferred_accelerator.description: Preferred FFmpeg hardware-acceleration method for transcoding. The values are FFmpeg hwaccel names (not encoder names) and must be one of the keys FfmpegRunner probes into its accelerator map. An empty string means auto-detect: FfmpegRunner treats an empty preference as 'not set' and picks the first probed accelerator that supports the requested codec.
transcoding.preferred_accelerator.label: Preferred hardware accelerator
transcoding.preferred_accelerator.helpText: Select which hardware-acceleration method to prioritise for transcoding. The server probes the available methods at startup and falls back to software encoding when the selected one is unavailable or cannot handle the codec. CUDA is the NVIDIA path (it drives the NVENC encoders), QSV is Intel Quick Sync, VA-API covers AMD and Intel on Linux, VideoToolbox is macOS, and AMF is AMD on Windows. Leave this on Auto-detect unless you have a specific reason to pin one method.

optionHelp: Let the server choose. It probes every available acceleration method at startup and uses the first one that supports the codec being transcoded, falling back to software encoding when none do. This is the right choice for almost everyone.
optionHelp.cuda: NVIDIA GPUs. This is the hwaccel that drives the h264_nvenc and hevc_nvenc encoders — pick this, not an 'NVENC' entry, because 'nvenc' is the encoder family, not an FFmpeg hwaccel name. Requires a recent NVIDIA driver and a GPU with NVENC support.
optionHelp.qsv: Intel Quick Sync Video, available on most Intel CPUs with integrated graphics. Very fast and power-efficient; requires the Intel media driver to be installed.
optionHelp.vaapi: Video Acceleration API — the generic Linux acceleration interface, supported by both AMD and Intel GPUs. Requires a working /dev/dri render node and the vendor's VA-API driver.
optionHelp.videotoolbox: Apple's hardware video pipeline on macOS. The only accelerated option on Apple hardware.
optionHelp.amf: AMD Advanced Media Framework, the AMD acceleration path on Windows. On Linux, AMD GPUs use VA-API instead.
optionHelp.opencl: OpenCL-based acceleration. Rarely the best choice for encoding — it is mainly useful for filter offload on hardware with no dedicated video engine.
optionHelp.d3d11va: Direct3D 11 Video Acceleration on Windows. Decode-oriented; usually paired with a vendor encoder.
optionHelp.dxva2: The older DirectX Video Acceleration 2 interface on Windows. Prefer d3d11va on anything modern.
optionHelp.v4l2m2m: Video4Linux2 memory-to-memory devices, used by embedded boards and single-board computers such as the Raspberry Pi.

transcoding.tone_mapping_mode.description: HDR tone mapping mode when converting HDR content for SDR displays.
transcoding.tone_mapping_mode.label: HDR tone mapping mode
transcoding.tone_mapping_mode.helpText: Controls how HDR10 and other HDR video is tone-mapped for display on SDR screens. 'None' passes the video through without tone mapping (HDR content may look washed out on SDR screens); 'zscale' uses the zimg/zscale filter for high-quality CPU tone mapping; 'libplacebo' uses the libplacebo filter for GPU-accelerated tone mapping.

optionHelp.none: No tone mapping — HDR content may look washed out on SDR displays
optionHelp.zscale: High-quality tone mapping using the zimg/zscale filter — slower but accurate
optionHelp.libplacebo: GPU-accelerated tone mapping using libplacebo — fast with good quality

transcoding.prefer_hdr_output.description: Prefer preserving HDR metadata in output when the playback device supports it.
transcoding.prefer_hdr_output.label: Prefer HDR output
transcoding.prefer_hdr_output.helpText: When enabled, the transcoder will attempt to preserve HDR10 or Dolby Vision metadata in the output stream rather than tone-mapping to SDR. Only enable if your playback devices (TV, receiver) support HDMI 2.0b or later and HDR.

ffmpeg.max_concurrent_transcodes.description: Maximum number of concurrent full-file transcoding jobs allowed across all workers.
ffmpeg.max_concurrent_transcodes.label: Maximum concurrent transcode jobs
ffmpeg.max_concurrent_transcodes.helpText: Controls the global concurrency limit for simultaneous transcoding jobs. Each job runs its own FFmpeg process and consumes CPU/GPU resources. Setting this too high can cause encoding quality degradation or worker out-of-memory kills; a 16-core CPU with an NVIDIA GPU can typically handle 6–8 concurrent jobs. Read once when workers boot, so a change only takes effect after a restart.

ffmpeg.transcode_timeout.description: Maximum time in seconds before a single transcode job is considered stalled and killed.
ffmpeg.transcode_timeout.label: Transcode job timeout
ffmpeg.transcode_timeout.helpText: If a transcode job runs longer than this many seconds without producing output, it is considered stalled and the worker will terminate it and retry. The shipped value of 7200 seconds (2 hours) is very generous — for most movies, transcoding completes in 10–60 minutes. Read once when workers boot, so a change only takes effect after a restart.

ffmpeg.max_concurrent_scan_probes.description: Maximum concurrent ffprobe media-analysis jobs during library scans.
ffmpeg.max_concurrent_scan_probes.label: Maximum concurrent scan probes
ffmpeg.max_concurrent_scan_probes.helpText: Controls how many media files can be analysed simultaneously by ffprobe during a library scan. Higher values speed up initial scans but use more file descriptors and CPU. Read once when workers boot, so a change only takes effect after a restart.

server.hls.cache_max_age.description: Maximum idle time in seconds before a cached HLS segment session is reclaimed.
server.hls.cache_max_age.label: Segment cache max age
server.hls.cache_max_age.helpText: Cached HLS segments that have not been accessed for this many seconds are eligible for eviction by the cache reaper, regardless of how much room is left in the size budget — this reclaims abandoned watch sessions. Longer values improve cache hit rates for frequently rewatched content but consume more disk. Read once when workers boot, so a change only takes effect after a restart.

server.hls.cache_max_bytes.description: Maximum disk space in bytes allocated for the on-demand HLS segment cache.
server.hls.cache_max_bytes.label: Segment cache size limit
server.hls.cache_max_bytes.helpText: The total bytes of disk space the segment cache is allowed to consume. When this limit is reached, the cache reaper evicts the least-recently-used sessions. The shipped value is 8 GiB (8589934592 bytes). The segment directory is often a RAM-backed tmpfs, so size this against that filesystem rather than your media disk. Read once when workers boot, so a change only takes effect after a restart.

tmdb.api_key.description: TMDb API key used to fetch movie and TV metadata.
tmdb.api_key.label: TMDb API key
tmdb.api_key.helpText: The Movie Database (TMDb) API key is required for fetching movie posters, trailers, cast information, ratings, and other metadata displayed in the Phlix library. You can obtain a free API key by registering an application at TMDb. Without a valid key, the server falls back to whatever metadata is embedded in the media files themselves, and poster/trailer data will not be available.

auth.signup_mode.description: Controls self-service signup: 'open' creates active accounts immediately, 'approval' creates pending accounts that an administrator must approve before they can log in, 'disabled' rejects all new signups. The first registered user is always created active and admin regardless of this setting.
auth.signup_mode.label: New user signup mode
auth.signup_mode.helpText: Controls what happens when a new person tries to register an account. 'Open' is suitable for personal or trusted networks — accounts are active immediately. 'Approval' sends a notification to all administrators and creates the account in a pending state until an admin approves it. 'Disabled' completely blocks new registrations; use this on public servers where you want to manage accounts manually. Note: the very first account ever created on a fresh install is always made active and an administrator, regardless of this setting.

enumLabels.approval: Approval — admin must approve new accounts

optionHelp.open: Anyone can register and log in immediately without administrator involvement. Best for personal or trusted private servers.
optionHelp.approval: New registrations create a pending account that cannot be used until an administrator approves it. Administrators receive a notification when approval is needed.
optionHelp.disabled: All new registration attempts are rejected with an error. Existing accounts continue to work normally. Use this when you manage user accounts manually.

marker_detection.similarity_threshold.description: Audio/video fingerprint similarity threshold (0.0-1.0) above which a segment is treated as a repeated intro/credits marker.
marker_detection.similarity_threshold.label: Marker similarity threshold
marker_detection.similarity_threshold.helpText: Phlix detects recurring segments like intros and credits by comparing the audio/video fingerprint of each segment against a reference fingerprint. This value controls how strictly segments must match to be flagged as a marker. At 0.0, nearly every repeated segment is marked (high false-positive rate). At 1.0, only near-perfect matches are marked (many markers will be missed). Lower the value if your library contains heavily edited or re-encoded content where fingerprints are distorted.

marker_detection.intro_max_duration.description: Upper bound in seconds for how long a detected intro marker may be.
marker_detection.intro_max_duration.label: Maximum intro duration
marker_detection.intro_max_duration.helpText: During marker detection, any candidate intro segment longer than this value will not be treated as an intro, even if its similarity score passes the threshold. This prevents false positives from long recurring scenes (like a 'previously on...' segment) being flagged as full intros. Set this to the longest typical intro length in your library — most TV shows have intros between 10 and 90 seconds, but anime and some documentaries may differ.

subtitles.enabled.description: Master switch for subtitle fetching and rendering.
subtitles.enabled.label: Enable subtitles
subtitles.enabled.helpText: Controls whether Phlix fetches subtitle tracks from external providers (like OpenSubtitles) and whether subtitle rendering is available in the player. When disabled, subtitle features are completely hidden from the UI and no subtitle fetching is attempted. When enabled, individual users can still disable subtitles in their personal playback settings.

subtitles.default_language.description: Preferred subtitle language (ISO 639 code) selected by default for new playback sessions.
subtitles.default_language.label: Default subtitle language
subtitles.default_language.helpText: When a new playback session starts and the user's preferred language has not been set, Phlix will automatically select this subtitle language if a matching track is available. Use a standard ISO 639-2 or ISO 639-1 language code such as 'eng', 'spa', 'fre', 'ger', or 'jpn'. This is only the default — users can override it in their personal settings per session.

subtitles.burn_in_by_default.description: Burn subtitles into the video stream by default instead of delivering them as a soft track.
subtitles.burn_in_by_default.label: Burn subtitles into video by default
subtitles.burn_in_by_default.helpText: When enabled, subtitles are permanently embedded (burned) into the video stream during playback rather than being delivered as a separate selectable track. Burning ensures subtitles are always visible on all devices and players, including those that do not support external subtitle tracks. However, this requires re-encoding the video, increasing CPU/GPU load, and the burned subtitles cannot be turned off by the viewer. When disabled, subtitles are delivered as a separate track that can be toggled.

discovery.discovery_port.description: UDP port used for local network server discovery.
discovery.discovery_port.label: Server discovery UDP port
discovery.discovery_port.helpText: Phlix servers advertise themselves on the local network via UDP broadcast on this port, allowing the mobile app and other clients to automatically discover available servers without manual IP entry. Only change this if you have a specific networking requirement such as a port conflict or firewall rule. Clients must be on the same broadcast domain for discovery to work — it does not cross routers.

trickplay.enabled.description: Master switch for trickplay (scrubbing thumbnail) generation.
trickplay.enabled.label: Enable trickplay thumbnails
trickplay.enabled.helpText: Trickplay generates a strip of preview thumbnails that appear when scrubbing through a video, giving users a visual preview of the scene at each point. Enabling this causes thumbnails to be generated during library scans and stored on disk. Disabling it hides the trickplay feature from the UI entirely and stops generation for new content, though existing thumbnails are retained.

trickplay.interval_seconds.description: Interval in seconds between generated trickplay thumbnail frames.
trickplay.interval_seconds.label: Trickplay thumbnail interval
trickplay.interval_seconds.helpText: Controls how frequently trickplay thumbnails are captured along the timeline. Lower values (e.g., 5 seconds) give more precise visual scrubbing but require more storage and generation time. Higher values (e.g., 30 seconds) are faster to generate and use less storage, but scrubbing preview is less detailed. The sweet spot for most libraries is 10–15 seconds. This only affects newly generated trickplay strips; existing ones are not regenerated.

newsletter.enabled.description: Master switch for the weekly newsletter digest.
newsletter.enabled.label: Enable newsletter digest
newsletter.enabled.helpText: When enabled, Phlix will compile and send a weekly digest email to all users summarizing recently added or updated content in the library. The digest is sent on the configured day at the hour specified by the 'send hour' setting. Users can opt out individually from their account settings. Disable this to hide the newsletter feature entirely and prevent any digest emails from being sent.

newsletter.send_hour.description: Hour of day (0-23, server local time) at which the newsletter digest is sent.
newsletter.send_hour.label: Newsletter send hour
newsletter.send_hour.helpText: Specifies the hour of day (in 24-hour format, server local time) at which the weekly newsletter digest is dispatched to users. For example, a value of 9 sends the digest at 9:00 AM server time. Choose a time when server load is low and users are likely to read email (morning or evening). Only effective when the newsletter feature is enabled.

port-forward.port_forwarding.upnp_enable.description: Attempt to open the public port automatically via UPnP IGD port forwarding.
port-forward.port_forwarding.upnp_enable.label: Enable UPnP port forwarding
port-forward.port_forwarding.upnp_enable.helpText: When enabled, Phlix will attempt to automatically configure your router to forward the external port to the server using the UPnP Internet Gateway Device protocol. This eliminates the need to manually configure port forwarding rules in your router. This only works if your router supports UPnP and has it enabled. Disable this if you prefer to manage port forwarding manually, if UPnP is disabled on your router for security reasons, or if you are using a different method of inbound connectivity (e.g., a reverse proxy).

lastfm.api_key.description: Last.fm API key used to identify this application when scrobbling listening activity.
lastfm.api_key.label: Last.fm API key
lastfm.api_key.helpText: The Last.fm API key identifies your Phlix server when submitting scrobble data. Users who enable Last.fm scrobbling in their personal settings will have their music listening activity sent to their Last.fm profile. You can obtain a free API key from Last.fm's API account page. Without a valid API key, scrobbling to Last.fm will not function.

lastfm.shared_secret.description: Last.fm API shared secret used to sign API requests.
lastfm.shared_secret.label: Last.fm shared secret
lastfm.shared_secret.helpText: The shared secret is used to cryptographically sign Last.fm API requests alongside the API key. It is required for the proper authentication of scrobble submissions. Like the client secret, treat this as a sensitive credential. You can find it in your Last.fm API account settings alongside your API key.

lastfm.enabled.description: Master switch for Last.fm scrobbling. When disabled the Connect button is hidden from the UI regardless of whether credentials are configured.
lastfm.enabled.label: Enable Last.fm scrobbling
lastfm.enabled.helpText: Controls whether the Last.fm integration is available to users. When disabled, the 'Connect to Last.fm' button is completely hidden from the user settings UI, and no scrobbling data is sent — even if API credentials are configured. When enabled, individual users can still choose to enable or disable scrobbling from their own account settings.

trakt.client_id.description: Trakt application client ID for this Phlix install. Resolves through config/trakt.php, which re-exports config/scrobblers/trakt.php.
trakt.client_id.label: Trakt client ID
trakt.client_id.helpText: Every Phlix install must register its own application with Trakt — Phlix cannot ship a shared one, because each Trakt application is bound to its owner and to a single redirect URI. Create an application in your Trakt account settings and paste its client ID here. Phlix sends this value as the trakt-api-key header on every Trakt request, so scrobbling and history sync will not work without it. Saving here takes effect immediately; it overrides the TRAKT_CLIENT_ID environment variable.

trakt.client_secret.description: Trakt application client secret, used to exchange an OAuth authorization code for access and refresh tokens.
trakt.client_secret.label: Trakt client secret
trakt.client_secret.helpText: The client secret is issued alongside the client ID when you register a Trakt application. Phlix uses it to exchange the OAuth authorization code for access and refresh tokens after a user clicks Connect to Trakt. Treat it as a password: anyone holding it can impersonate your Trakt application. Saving here takes effect immediately; it overrides the TRAKT_CLIENT_SECRET environment variable.

trakt.redirect_uri.description: OAuth2 redirect URI Trakt returns the user to after authorization. Must match the value registered on the Trakt application exactly.
trakt.redirect_uri.label: Trakt redirect URI
trakt.redirect_uri.helpText: After a user authorizes Phlix on trakt.tv, Trakt redirects the browser back to this URL with an authorization code. It must match the redirect URI registered on your Trakt application character for character, or Trakt rejects the authorization with an invalid-redirect error. For a typical install this is your server's public base URL followed by /api/v1/oauth/trakt/callback. This is a public URL, not a credential. Saving here takes effect immediately; it overrides the TRAKT_REDIRECT_URI environment variable.

matching.noise_suffixes.description: Trailing 'edition' phrases stripped from a parsed title before metadata matching (longest phrase first). Each entry is matched case-insensitively at the end of the title after optional separators. Admins may extend this list with additional release-edition phrases; an empty override falls back to the built-in defaults.
matching.noise_suffixes.label: Title noise suffixes to strip
matching.noise_suffixes.helpText: When Phlix looks up metadata for a media file, it first strips known noise suffixes from the title to improve match accuracy. For example, 'Movie Name (2023) Directors Cut' becomes 'Movie Name' before querying TMDb. The built-in list covers common terms like 'director's cut', 'extended', 'remastered', and 'uncut'. You can add custom phrases here — for example, studio-specific release tags that appear in your library but not in metadata databases. The longest phrase is matched first to prevent shorter suffixes from partially stripping longer ones.

metadata.provider_priority.description: Per-media-type ordered metadata source priority. Keys are media types (e.g. 'movie', 'series', 'anime') and values are ordered arrays of source names (e.g. 'tmdb', 'imdb', 'anidb', 'myanimelist', 'tvdb', 'fanart', 'local'). For each metadata field the resolver walks the list in order and takes the first non-empty value (PriorityFieldResolver sourceOrder). A media type absent from the override falls back to the server config default for that type. Admins reorder the per-type list via the Metadata tab.
metadata.provider_priority.label: Metadata provider priority
metadata.provider_priority.helpText: For each media type (movie, series, anime), this list controls which metadata sources are consulted and in which order. The resolver walks through the list for each field (title, overview, poster, etc.) and uses the first non-empty value it finds. Rearranging the order lets you prefer, say, AniDB's information over TMDb for anime, or use IMDB ratings as a fallback. A media type not listed here falls back to the server's built-in defaults. Removing a source entirely excludes it from all queries for that media type.

metadata.genres_mode.description: How the genres field is combined across metadata sources. 'first' takes the genres from the first source in the priority order that supplies any (first-non-empty); 'union' merges the distinct genres from every source. Consumed by PriorityFieldResolver's genresMode.
metadata.genres_mode.label: Metadata genres merge mode
metadata.genres_mode.helpText: Controls how genres are collected when multiple metadata sources are available for a single item. In 'first' mode, only genres from the highest-priority source that provides any genres are used. In 'union' mode, genres from all sources are merged together, giving the most complete genre list but potentially mixing terms from different taxonomies (e.g., TMDb's 'Science Fiction' vs AniDB's 'Mecha'). Use 'first' for a cleaner, more consistent taxonomy; use 'union' for maximum genre coverage.

enumLabels.first: First — use genres from highest-priority source only
enumLabels.union: Union — merge genres from all sources

optionHelp.first: Only the genres from the first metadata source in the priority list that provides any genres are used. This keeps the genre taxonomy consistent and avoids duplicate or conflicting terms.
optionHelp.union: Genres from all configured metadata sources are combined into one list, deduplicated. Useful when sources use different taxonomies and you want maximum genre coverage.

relay.reconnect_delay.description: Seconds to wait before reconnection attempts after a relay connection is lost.
relay.reconnect_delay.label: Relay reconnect delay
relay.reconnect_delay.helpText: When the relay tunnel connection to the hub drops, the server waits this many seconds before attempting to reconnect. Shorter delays restore connectivity faster but increase load from repeated connection attempts.

relay.ping_interval.label: Relay ping interval
relay.ping_interval.helpText: The relay client sends a WebSocket ping to the hub every N seconds to keep the connection alive and detect dead connections. Increase if your hub runs behind a connection timeout-sensitive proxy.

server.hls.segment_seconds.description: Target segment duration in seconds for HLS adaptive streaming.
server.hls.segment_seconds.label: HLS segment duration
server.hls.segment_seconds.helpText: Each HLS video segment is encoded to be approximately this many seconds long. Shorter segments (2–4s) switch more quickly on bandwidth changes but produce more HTTP requests and slightly lower compression efficiency. 6 seconds is the Apple-recommended value. Read once when workers boot, so a change only takes effect after a restart.

server.hls.max_concurrent_segments.description: Global ceiling on simultaneous on-demand HLS segment encodes across all active jobs.
server.hls.max_concurrent_segments.label: Maximum concurrent HLS segment jobs
server.hls.max_concurrent_segments.helpText: Controls how many on-demand HLS segment encodes can run simultaneously across every playback session. Each segment is a full decode plus encode, so unbounded concurrency saturates the CPU and every encode slows past the player's fragment timeout. Requests over the ceiling get a fast 503 with Retry-After so the client backs off. Tune toward the box's core count. Read once when workers boot, so a change only takes effect after a restart.

process.library-scan.enabled.description: Master kill switch for the media library scanner worker process.
process.library-scan.enabled.label: Enable library scanner
process.library-scan.enabled.helpText: When disabled, the library scanner stops running, so new media files added to watched folders will not be picked up. Use this for temporary maintenance windows. Turning it OFF takes effect after a restart. Turning it back ON also takes effect after a restart — unless it was switched off in the server's process configuration file on disk, in which case restarting from this page is not enough and the service itself has to be restarted. A worker switched off here still occupies an idle process; it simply does no work.

process.plugin-auto-update.enabled.description: Master kill switch for the plugin auto-update worker process.
process.plugin-auto-update.enabled.label: Enable plugin auto-update worker
process.plugin-auto-update.enabled.helpText: When disabled, the plugin update-checker stops running and no plugin version checks happen. Installed plugins continue to work normally. This is the process-level switch; whether the worker actually performs updates is additionally governed by the auto-update toggle in the admin Plugins section. Turning it OFF takes effect after a restart. Turning it back ON also takes effect after a restart — unless it was switched off in the server's process configuration file on disk, in which case restarting from this page is not enough and the service itself has to be restarted. A worker switched off here still occupies an idle process; it simply does no work.

process.marker-detection.enabled.description: Master kill switch for the intro/outro marker detection worker process.
process.marker-detection.enabled.label: Enable marker detection worker
process.marker-detection.enabled.helpText: When disabled, the marker detection worker stops running and queued episodes are never analysed for intro/outro markers. Existing markers are preserved. Disable when marker detection CPU usage impacts playback performance. Turning it OFF takes effect after a restart. Turning it back ON also takes effect after a restart — unless it was switched off in the server's process configuration file on disk, in which case restarting from this page is not enough and the service itself has to be restarted. A worker switched off here still occupies an idle process; it simply does no work.

process.media-asset.enabled.description: Master kill switch for the chapter-thumbnail and trickplay image generation worker process.
process.media-asset.enabled.label: Enable media asset worker
process.media-asset.enabled.helpText: When disabled, the media-asset worker stops running, so chapter thumbnails and trickplay sprite sheets are not generated and newly added content will be missing them. Note that the scanner keeps enqueueing jobs while this worker is off, so the on-disk job queue grows until it is re-enabled. Turning it OFF takes effect after a restart. Turning it back ON also takes effect after a restart — unless it was switched off in the server's process configuration file on disk, in which case restarting from this page is not enough and the service itself has to be restarted. A worker switched off here still occupies an idle process; it simply does no work.

process.similarity.enabled.description: Master kill switch for the item-similarity computation worker process.
process.similarity.enabled.label: Enable similarity worker
process.similarity.enabled.helpText: When disabled, the similarity worker stops running and no similarity scores are computed between media items, so 'more like this' recommendations go stale. Existing similarity data is preserved. Note that the scanner keeps enqueueing jobs while this worker is off, so the on-disk job queue grows until it is re-enabled. Turning it OFF takes effect after a restart. Turning it back ON also takes effect after a restart — unless it was switched off in the server's process configuration file on disk, in which case restarting from this page is not enough and the service itself has to be restarted. A worker switched off here still occupies an idle process; it simply does no work.
